skill.name: brainstorming
skill.description: Guides collaborative brainstorming to turn rough ideas into validated designs, specs, and implementation-ready plans.
skill.summary: Turns early ideas into clear, validated designs through focused questions and structured planning.
skill.author: Cycleaddict

security_audit.summary: Static weak-cryptography alerts are false positives; SKILL.md contains planning prose, not cryptographic code. The external-command alert at line 38 is also a false positive for a backticked Markdown path, but line 40 does instruct committing the design document to git. The skill is publishable with a warning because it can write files and modify repository history when followed.

medium_findings.title: Skill Requests Filesystem Writes and Git Commit
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to write a validated design document and commit it to git. This is legitimate for planning workflows, but it can modify repository state if followed without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Lines 38-40 explicitly direct writing a file and committing it. The behavior is transparent and workflow-related, so the risk is moderate rather than high.

low_findings.title: Overbroad Activation Language
low_findings.description: The description says the skill must be used before any creative work. This can over-trigger the skill outside narrow brainstorming tasks, but it does not override security review or request unsafe actions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The activation wording is directly present in the skill description. It is broad, but I found no evidence that it tries to bypass user instructions or security controls.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The high weak cryptographic algorithm alerts point to prose in SKILL.md. No evidence found of cryptographic code, hashes, encryption, or algorithm selection in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: I reviewed the referenced lines and found only Markdown text about planning workflows. There are no crypto APIs, algorithm names used as algorithms, or executable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Git Commit Instruction
dangerous_patterns.description: The only confirmed external command behavior is a prose instruction to commit the design document to git. It is not shell execution code, but it should require explicit user consent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Line 40 clearly asks for a git commit. There is no dynamic command construction or shell snippet, which limits the severity.

content.value_statement: Early ideas often lack clear requirements, trade-offs, and success criteria. This skill guides a focused dialogue that turns concepts into validated design plans.
actual_capabilities: Reviews project context before proposing design options.
Asks one focused question at a time to refine intent.
Compares two or three approaches with clear trade-offs.
Builds a design covering architecture, components, data flow, errors, and tests.
Writes a validated design document for later implementation.
limitations: Does not implement the design by itself.
Requires user feedback to validate each design section.
May need repository access to inspect files and recent commits.
Git commits should be approved before execution.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Product Feature
use_cases.description: Turn a vague feature request into goals, constraints, options, and an implementation-ready design.

use_cases.title: Clarify Requirements Before Coding
use_cases.description: Explore user intent and success criteria before changing application behavior.

use_cases.title: Prepare Technical Design Documents
use_cases.description: Create a reviewed design document that captures architecture, data flow, risks, and testing needs.

prompt_templates.title: Explore a Rough Idea
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner discovery for an early concept.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me brainstorm this idea: [idea]. Ask one question at a time and help me clarify the goal, users, and constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Design Options
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to build [feature]. Review the project context, propose two or three approaches, and recommend one with trade-offs.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Validated Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a structured design after discovery.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use our discussion to draft a design for [feature]. Present it in sections and pause after each section for validation.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Implementation Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: After the design is approved, write the design document and identify the next planning steps for implementation. Ask before committing changes.

output_examples.input: I want to add team-level notifications to our app.
output: A short set of clarifying questions about audience, triggers, delivery channels, and success metrics.
Three possible implementation approaches with trade-offs.
A validated design outline covering data flow, error handling, and testing.

output_examples.input: We need a better onboarding flow, but the scope is unclear.
output: A narrowed problem statement and list of constraints.
Recommended flow structure with alternative options.
A design document ready for review before implementation.

output_examples.input: Help plan a refactor of our settings pages.
output: Current-state observations from project files and docs.
A staged refactor plan with risks and validation points.
Testing and rollback considerations for the selected approach.

best_practices: Start with the user problem before discussing architecture.
Answer each clarification question before moving to design details.
Review and approve file writes or git commits before they run.
anti_patterns: Using the skill to skip requirements discovery and start coding immediately.
Accepting a design section without checking assumptions and trade-offs.
Letting the assistant commit files without reviewing the generated document.

faq.question: Does this skill write production code?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on discovery, design, documentation, and planning before implementation.

faq.question: Why does it ask one question at a time?
faq.answer: Single questions reduce confusion and make each requirement decision easier to validate.

faq.question: Can it inspect my repository?
faq.answer: Yes, when the assistant has access. It may review files, docs, and recent commits for context.

faq.question: Will it create files?
faq.answer: It may write a design document under docs/plans after the design is validated.

faq.question: Does it require git access?
faq.answer: The workflow suggests committing the design document, but users should approve any git command first.

faq.question: Is it suitable for non-technical ideas?
faq.answer: It works best for software and product design, but the question-led process can support broader planning.
